summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – EX TEMPORE REASONS – PARENTING – INTERIM PROCEEDINGS – Where the Father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) made oral applications for interim orders in circumstances where the final hearing is adjourned part-heard – Where the single expert has been discharged and the Family Report struck out – Where a new single expert has been appointed and a new Family Report will be completed – Where the children have not spent time with the Father for two years – Where the Mother is pursuing a “no time” case – Where the ICL seeks interim orders for a reportable therapeutic process for the Father and the children – Where the Father seeks interim orders for a therapeutic reintroduction process following which supervised time with the children commences – Where the Mother opposes the making of further interim orders – Where orders made for the parents and the children to engage in a reportable therapeutic process for the purpose of exploring the reintroduction of the children to their Father – Where no orders made as to frequency or length of appointments – Where interim orders providing for the children to spend no time with the Father remain in force – Where liberty to relist upon the provision of a report from the therapeutic process – Where orders made amending an injunction against the Father with respect to his proximity to the children’s school.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

BACKGROUND

These brief ex tempore Reasons deal with oral applications for interim parenting orders made by the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) and the father earlier today. The oral applications were made on the seventh day of the final hearing of this parenting matter, which concerns X, aged six and Y, aged five. The lay evidence was concluded on 13 February, and the matter was adjourned part-heard to 16 and 17 March for the purpose of hearing expert evidence and closing submissions.
The oral applications were made in circumstances where I have earlier today made consent orders for the discharge of the single expert, Dr G, in this matter and for the Family Report prepared by her, which had previously been received into evidence, to be struck out. The single expert was appointed by consent of the parties pursuant to r 7.03 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth). I am satisfied that, having regard to the various powers which the Court has in respect of single experts, including as to their appointment, I have the power to discharge a single expert, notwithstanding that I am not given that power explicitly. After considering the Family Report, an adversarial report of Mr K which I admitted into evidence on application by the father and which evidence was not challenged, and hearing Dr G give her oral evidence under cross examination by counsel for the ICL and counsel for the mother, I was satisfied that it is in the interests of justice (because it is necessary to enable me to make a decision which is in the children’s best interests) for her to be discharged and for her report to be struck out.
I have also made consent orders for the appointment of a new single expert, and by which the matter is again adjourned part heard to 16 November, by which date a new Family Report will be available. The question raised by the oral applications is what is to happen in the meantime – a period of at least a further eight months.
X and Y have not spent time with their father for two years. In August 2025, I made interim orders for no time, relying heavily on the Family Report which has now been struck out.
On 11 February 2026, by consent I made final orders providing for their mother to have sole parental responsibility for X and Y, and for them to live with her. The remaining issues concern what time, if any, the children spend with their father.
Their mother seeks final orders for no time. Their father seeks a therapeutic reintroduction, followed by supervised time and then a progression ultimately to equal time. The ICL has not yet finalised her position as to final orders.

FACTUAL FINDINGS

It is the mother’s evidence in these proceedings that she has been subjected to a course of controlling and coercive conduct by the father during the relationship, after separation and during these proceedings. It is her evidence that the children have been present at times during the use of family violence by the father, and have been verbally, emotionally and psychologically abused by him at times.
The father has acknowledged that he has at times used family violence (including of a controlling nature) towards the mother. Since the commencement of these proceedings, he has engaged in therapy with two therapists (one of whom was on affidavit), has commenced a men’s behavioural change program and is working with a “life coach”.
As was made clear in oral submissions in support of the oral applications for interim orders, the mother puts in issue in the proceedings the extent to which the father has been honest about and has insight into his behaviour. The father puts in issue in the proceedings whether the mother is genuine in her concerns about the children spending time with him, given that she left the children in the care of the father, including for the purposes of going on holidays right up until she left the former matrimonial home.
In due course, and following careful review of the evidence, hearing any further evidence, including the expert evidence, and hearing and considering closing submissions, I will make the necessary findings.
At this stage, I can make the following relevant findings based on the evidence of the parents’ respective treating physicians that:
(1) The father is significantly engaged with professionals who are assisting him in relation to his mental health and behaviour. I accept the evidence of his psychologist that he is appropriately engaged in therapy, and has achieved some greater insight into the impact of his behaviour;

(2) The mother has post-traumatic stress disorder, but that is relatively well managed and she is well supported in dealing with it.

INTERIM ORDERS SOUGHT

The interim orders now sought by the ICL and the father provide for a therapeutic process, including reintroduction of the children to the father in therapy subject to the discretion of the therapist. The father, but not the ICL, seeks that the interim orders progress beyond that therapeutic process to provide for supervised time after a period of 6-12 weeks of therapy.
The ICL’s orders sought as her primary position envisaged the possibility that the family therapist proposed may decide to introduce the children to their father in therapy, but counsel for the ICL acknowledged that that may involve a delegation of judicial power, and so proposed as the ICL’s secondary position that the therapeutic process start and that the parties and the ICL have liberty to relist upon receipt of a report of that process.
The mother opposes the making of any further interim parenting orders, and submissions made on her behalf broadly raise the undesirability for the children of commencing a process which may at least be difficult for the children, in a context where the mother’s position remains that this is a “no time” case and where if I made those orders, the children would likely be, at least, confused. Her “fall back” position was that I would make orders in accordance with the ICL’s secondary position (that is, to start therapy but not time).

DETERMINATION

For the reasons set out below, I make orders broadly in accordance with the ICL’s secondary position.
I decline to make the orders sought as the primary position by the ICL because I cannot delegate the decision about whether or not the children spend time with the father to a clinician (based on the authority of Lainhart & Ellinson [2023] FedCFamC1A 200; (2023) FLC 94-166).
I decline to make the orders sought by the father, that is, either to move straight to supervised time or to progress after a period of therapy to supervised time, because I am not satisfied at this stage, and in the absence of a Family Report, and in circumstances where the children have not now spent time with their father for two years, that the commencement of supervised time on an interim basis is in the children’s best interests – in the sense that it is an arrangement which promotes their emotional and psychological safety. The ICL opposed the making of such an order in circumstances where there is a lacuna in the evidence – including where it is yet to be seen how the children cope with even the idea of being reintroduced to their father. I accept that submission.
I am, however, satisfied that it is in the children’s best interests to commence a process of reportable therapy for the children and their father, with a view to exploring the reintroduction of the children to their father.
I am sufficiently satisfied that a professional therapist will manage a process which is either emotionally and psychologically safe for the children, or is not continued. The children will not meet with their father at least at this stage, so there is no question that they or the mother will be subjected to any other safety concerns. The orders I make require that the appointments will be made in a way which ensures the mother and children do not come into contact with the father. I am satisfied that the mother will obtain the assistance she needs to manage the process such that she and the children are not significantly adversely affected by what I expect will be her anxiety about facilitating it.
The orders I make are therefore consistent with the children’s and their mother’s safety from harm and will otherwise serve the children’s best interests in the following ways:
(a) First, the process provided for will at least prepare the children for the possibility that they will recommence spending time with the father, and will likely improve their ability to transition to such time if it is ultimately ordered.

(b) Second, the process is likely to assist the father to have greater understanding of the children’s perspective on their relationship with him such that, if time is ordered, the children will benefit from that greater understanding.

(c) Third, the process may reveal that a different interim outcome is in the children’s best interests – that may be either the conclusion of therapy or the children commencing to spend time with their father.

(d) Fourth, if the process results in evidence that supports the commencement of the children’s time with their father, that will enable the single expert better to assess the children’s responses to their father and his interactions with them.

(e) Fifth, the process will provide some evidence which may assist the single expert in making her recommendations and me in making the decision about what final orders are in X and Y’s best interests.

To be clear, I decline to discharge my interim order made on 28 July 2025 that the children spend no time with the father. Necessarily then, therapy will not progress to the stage of the therapist seeing the children and their father together. The parties and the ICL have liberty to apply if there is evidence arising from the therapeutic process in support of a further change to the interim orders. Any such application will be listed before me.
The ICL seeks orders for therapy with a clinician based at a specialised clinic known as “[L Clinic]”. I have very limited evidence about L Clinic, but received into evidence some very brief information about it and about the principal, Mr J. On behalf of the mother, no alternate order was sought such that, for example, she would have the option to nominate three therapists and the father would choose one. There is an efficiency to the process proposed by the ICL and the father. In the event Mr J is not available, the parents and the ICL will need to agree an alternate therapist, whether that be from L Clinic or elsewhere. I decline to specify the frequency or length of appointments, as that should be at the discretion of the clinician.
The father also sought that I vary an injunctive order which I made on 28 July 2025 which restrains him from being within 500 metres of the children’s school. I made final property orders by consent on 12 February 2026, which provide for the father to retain the parties’ former matrimonial home, which I was told from the bar table is within that distance from the children’s school (being 70 metres directly or 250 metres walking distance). On behalf of the mother, the amendment was resisted on the basis that the parents may “bump into” each other as the mother takes the children to and from school. I was satisfied on 28 July that the injunction I made was appropriate for the welfare of X and Y. That was in circumstances where there was no need for the father to be within 500 metres of the school. The matter requires consideration given that the father will now own (and may live in) the former matrimonial home. I am satisfied that, particularly given the other restraints in Order 9, the fact that these proceedings are ongoing, and the evidence of the work that the father has done and is doing, that the issue is adequately addressed by an amendment to make the relevant distance in Order 9(a) 100 metres.
